1: "Praise the LORD, all nations! Praise him, all peoples of the earth!" — the shortest psalm in the Psalter, but theologically among the most momentous: a universal summons to all nations and peoples to worship YHWH. Paul quotes this verse directly in Romans 15:11 as part of a four-text catena proving that the Gentiles will glorify God through Christ: "'Praise the Lord, all you Gentiles; let all the peoples extol him'" (DIRECT CITATION). Romans 15:9-12 assembles Psalms 18:49, Deuteronomy 32:43, Psalms 117:1, and Isaiah 11:10 to demonstrate that the inclusion of the Gentiles is not an afterthought but the goal of Israel's entire covenant history. Psalms 117:1 sits at the climax of this argument: all nations praising YHWH is what the Messiah came to accomplish (Matthew 28:19-20; Acts 1:8; Revelation 7:9-10).
2: "For his steadfast love (hesed) toward us is great, and the faithfulness (emet) of the LORD endures forever. Praise the LORD!" — the Exodus 34:6-7 formula compressed to its essence: great love, enduring faithfulness. John 1:14: "the Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us. We have seen his glory, the glory of the one and only Son, who came from the Father, full of grace and truth." The hesed and emet of Psalms 117:2 are the charis and aletheia of the Incarnation — the steadfast love and faithfulness of YHWH become embodied in the person of Jesus Christ. Hebrews 13:8: "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever" — his faithfulness endures not just forever in the abstract but in his own eternal, unchanging person. The two-verse psalm that calls all nations to praise is grounded in the character of the God who comes in Christ: love that will not let go, faithfulness that will not fail.
